Zero-shot LLMs exhibited language-dependent instability and lower effectiveness compared to fine-tuned encoders.
The selective-verification layer improved accuracy from 71.5% to 84.4% by deferring low-confidence cases.
Achieved 72.3% coverage while maintaining a selective Macro-F1 of 0.76 in real-world clinical distributions.
Demonstrated robustness under both balanced and natural-prevalence clinical documentation patterns.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers develop IndicBERT-HPA, outperforming LLMs in multilingual orthopedic classification using language-aware adapters and verification-guided deferral mechanisms.

title: When Are Multimodal Predictions Biologically Supported? A Diagnostic Evaluation Framework
summary: Multimodal models in oncology can produce accurate predictions, but accurate prediction does not reveal whether the model has learned biology that is shared across modalities, biology confined to one modality, or spurious correlations that reflect confounders rather than genuine biology. We introduce DECAT, a model-agnostic post-hoc evaluation framework that classifies multimodal representations into four diagnostic scenarios for a given task and modality, using five null-referenced metrics and a rule-based decision procedure. The framework operates on learned representations, requires no knowledge of which specific confounder is present, and returns indeterminate when the evidence is insufficient. We validate DECAT on synthetic data across four multimodal model classes (over 2,500 trained representations) and on real data from 8,979 TCGA patients, evaluating both multimodal embeddings and five pretrained pathology foundation models. Entangled models (e.g., CLIP) achieve near-perfect shared biology detection but falsely claim shared biology in the majority of cases where it is absent on real foundation model embeddings. This false claim rate increases with confound strength so that larger cohorts and stronger representations produce more confident but still incorrect diagnoses. Applied to both multimodal TCGA embeddings and five pathology foundation models without paired RNA, DECAT detects confounding invisible to AUROC without requiring the confounder labels, as confirmed by post-hoc stratification.

processed.summary:
• Introduces **DECAT**, a model-agnostic post-hoc evaluation framework designed to determine if multimodal representations capture **shared biology** or **spurious correlations**.
• Employs a **rule-based decision procedure** using five null-referenced metrics to classify representations into four diagnostic scenarios without requiring explicit labels for confounders.
• Analyzes the behavior of **entangled models (e.g., CLIP)**, revealing that they often falsely claim shared biological signals in the presence of strong confounders despite high accuracy.
• Provides a robust methodology for **AI Evaluation** in high-stakes oncology tasks, where traditional metrics like AUROC fail to detect hidden data biases.
key_results: DECAT successfully detects confounding variables invisible to traditional AUROC metrics across 8,979 TCGA patients.
Entangled models like CLIP demonstrate near-perfect shared biology detection but high false positive rates when biology is absent.
False claim rates regarding shared biology increase proportionally with confounder strength and cohort size.
The framework validated effectively on over 2,500 trained representations and five pathology foundation models.
DECAT provides an 'indeterminate' status when evidence is insufficient, preventing overconfident but incorrect biological diagnoses.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: Harvard researchers introduce DECAT to distinguish genuine biological signals from spurious correlations in multimodal oncology foundation models.

title: General-purpose LLMs as Constrained Crystal Composition Generators
summary: The targeted discovery of inorganic materials remains challenging due to the vastness of compositional design spaces and the high cost of exhaustive screening. Task-specific generative artificial intelligence represents a particularly efficient alternative to screening, yet demands tedious collection of training data before providing real benefit. General-purpose large language models (LLMs) have recently shown tremendous potential for the targeted generation of single, optimal materials compositions without the need for task-specific fine-tuning. However, it is unclear whether LLMs generally pose an advantage compared to specialized generative models, in particular in large design spaces. Here, we demonstrate that such models are capable of covering entire regions of the targeted property space effectively and systematically. Using Elpasolite materials as an established benchmark for generative tasks in large chemical spaces, we find that an iterative prompt-and-response framework is able to recover on average 96% of all low-energy Elpasolites in the target region. This performance, driven mainly by iterative in-context learning, surpasses the generative abilities of previous, task-specific models. Our results establish general-purpose LLMs as flexible and accessible components for inverse materials design workflows.

processed.summary:
- Utilizes **general-purpose LLMs** within an **iterative prompt-and-response framework** to explore inorganic material design spaces without task-specific fine-tuning.
- Employs **iterative in-context learning** to refine search queries, allowing the model to systematically target low-energy crystal compositions in large chemical spaces.
- Demonstrates that **prompt engineering** and LLM reasoning can outperform specialized generative models previously considered state-of-the-art for material discovery.
- Integrates LLMs as flexible, accessible components for **inverse materials design**, significantly reducing the data collection burden for niche scientific tasks.
key_results: Achieved an average recovery rate of 96% for all low-energy Elpasolite materials in the target region.
Demonstrated that general-purpose LLMs can systematicially cover entire regions of a targeted property space.
Outperformed previous task-specific, specialized generative models in large chemical design spaces.
Validated iterative in-context learning as a primary driver for high-performance generative tasks.
Established a low-barrier workflow for crystal composition generation using standard LLM architectures.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers demonstrate that general-purpose LLMs outperform specialized models in discovering crystal compositions via iterative in-context learning.

title: An efficient Progressive Swapping to the Middle distribution protocol adapted to imperfect quantum memories in quantum networks
summary: The distribution of entangled pairs of photons on the links composing a quantum network, combined with Bell state measurements and teleportation, is the basic apparatus to transfer quantum bits (qubits) over long distances. Entanglement distribution establishes an end-to-end entangled pair while consuming intermediate pairs on links and holding them for a certain time period. The technical literature identifies two main kinds of protocols, parallel and sequential ones, the latter having an advantage in resource consumption over the former. In this paper, we introduce an efficient swapping protocol called Progressive Swapping to the Middle (PSM) as it combines the existing Progressive Swapping (PS) protocol from both extremities of a path that meet in the middle where the received pairs are swapped. We compare PSM with two parallel protocols and PS; in our evaluation, we take into account imperfect memories and fidelity degradation. We demonstrate that PSM yields a much better link probability than PS while keeping a reasonable link fidelity, and shows an advantage in resource consumption over other protocols.

processed.summary:
- Introduction of the **Progressive Swapping to the Middle (PSM)** protocol, which optimizes quantum entanglement distribution by merging pairs from both path extremities.
- The architecture specifically accounts for **imperfect quantum memories** and signal **fidelity degradation**, bridging the gap between parallel and sequential network protocols.
- Focuses on increasing **link probability** while maintaining lower resource consumption compared to existing parallel distribution frameworks.
key_results: Introduction of the Progressive Swapping to the Middle (PSM) protocol for quantum networks.
PSM yields significantly higher link probability compared to the standard Progressive Swapping (PS) protocol.
Demonstration that PSM maintains reasonable link fidelity even with hardware-induced memory errors.
Evidence of superior resource efficiency compared to traditional parallel distribution protocols.
Established a robust framework for end-to-end entanglement over long-distance links in noisy environments.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce the PSM protocol to optimize entanglement distribution and resource efficiency in imperfect quantum networks.

title: LinTree: Improving LLM Reasoning with Explicitly Structured Search Histories
summary: Large language models (LLMs) often solve reasoning problems by generating intermediate traces that explore and revise partial solutions. From a search perspective, these traces can be viewed as linearized search trees, where the model extends a partial solution, abandons it when it fails, and backtracks to try alternatives. Compared with traditional heuristic-guided search, such a policy has a potential advantage: it conditions on the whole search trace rather than only on the current local state. We first test whether LLMs utilize this advantage by comparing trace-conditioned reasoning policies against best-first search equipped with an LLM heuristic that only observes the current local state. Across three controlled reasoning environments, Blocks World, grid Navigation, and Sokoban, we find that raw access to search history alone is not enough to reliably outperform heuristic search. We then study one possible reason: in LLM reasoning traces, the underlying search tree is only implicitly represented, and when the model backtracks or switches branches, the trace does not explicitly identify which earlier search state is being revisited. We show that adding simple parent pointers to explicitly represent the linearized tree (LinTree) structure improves both task performance and search efficiency relative to implicit reasoning models and LLM-heuristic-guided search. These results suggest that search history becomes most useful when its tree structure is made explicit, motivating more structure-aware representations for LLM reasoning.

processed.summary:
• Proposes **LinTree**, a method to improve **LLM reasoning** by explicitly representing the underlying tree structure in linearized search traces.
 • Evaluates the effectiveness of **trace-conditioned policies** against **heuristic-guided search**, finding that raw search history alone is insufficient for superior performance.
 • Introduces **parent pointers** to clearly identify which previous states are being revisited during **backtracking**, reducing ambiguity in branch switching.
 • Demonstrates significant gains in **search efficiency** and task completion across structured reasoning environments like **Blocks World** and **Sokoban**.
key_results: Implicit search history fails to consistently outperform LLM-heuristic-guided best-first search.
Explicitly labeling tree structure using parent pointers improves task success rates.
LinTree significantly enhances search efficiency by clarifying state transitions in complex histories.
Structural awareness proves more critical for reasoning than simply providing raw historical context.
Performance gains were validated across three distinct environments: Blocks World, Navigation, and Sokoban.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce LinTree, using explicit parent pointers to boost LLM reasoning performance and search efficiency in complex tasks.

title: Are Full Rollouts Necessary for On-Policy Distillation?
summary: On-policy distillation (OPD) provides dense teacher feedback along rollouts generated by the student and has emerged as a promising post-training paradigm for long-horizon reasoning. However, standard OPD typically generates full rollouts during training, which is computationally expensive and may expose the student to unreliable teacher feedback at late rollout positions, especially during early training. We identify the rollout horizon as a key bottleneck in OPD that substantially impacts training efficiency. Unlike Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR), OPD does not require a complete trajectory or a final answer reward to provide learning signals. This observation suggests that full rollouts may not always be necessary for effective OPD. Motivated by this insight, we propose two simple horizon-control strategies: Progressive OPD (POPD), which gradually expands the rollout horizon during training, and Truncated OPD (TOPD), which permanently performs distillation on reliable truncated rollouts. Experiments on mathematical reasoning show that POPD improves the training efficiency of OPD by up to 3$\times$, while TOPD matches OPD performance using only 10\% of the rollout horizon, leading to substantial wall-clock and memory reductions. These results demonstrate that controlling the rollout horizon offers a simple and practical path to more efficient OPD.

processed.summary:
- Identifies the **rollout horizon** as a primary efficiency bottleneck in **On-Policy Distillation (OPD)** for reasoning models.
- Introduces **Progressive OPD (POPD)**, which dynamically scales the generation length during training to improve early-stage stability.
- Proposes **Truncated OPD (TOPD)**, demonstrating that training on short, reliable rollout segments is sufficient for learning complex reasoning.
- Achieves substantial reductions in **wall-clock time** and **memory usage** without sacrificing accuracy on mathematical reasoning benchmarks.
key_results: POPD improves training efficiency by up to 3x compared to standard distillation.
TOPD matches full-rollout performance using only 10% of the trajectory length.
Identifies that late-stage teacher feedback is often unreliable during early training phases.
Demonstrates significant memory and compute savings for post-training reasoning models.
Proves that dense learning signals do not require complete trajectories or final answer rewards.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: Tsinghua University researchers demonstrate that truncated rollouts in on-policy distillation significantly boost training efficiency for reasoning models.

title: BenHalluEval: A Multi-Task Hallucination Evaluation Framework for Large Language Models on Bengali
summary: Despite Bengali being the sixth most spoken language in the world, no prior work has systematically evaluated hallucination in large language models (LLMs) for Bengali. We introduce BenHalluEval, a fine-grained hallucination evaluation framework for Bengali covering four tasks: Generative Question Answering (GQA), Bangla-English Code-Mixed QA, Summarization, and Reasoning. We construct 12,000 hallucinated candidates using GPT-5.4 across twelve task-specific hallucination types, drawn from three existing Bengali datasets, and evaluate seven LLMs spanning reasoning-oriented, multilingual, and Bengali-centric categories under a dual-track protocol that independently measures false-positive rate on ground-truth instances (Track A) and hallucination detection rate on hallucinated candidates (Track B). To jointly penalise both failure modes and prevent inflated scores from uniform response bias, we propose BenHalluScore, a dual-track calibration metric that ranges from 7.72% to 55.42% across models and tasks, revealing substantial variation in hallucination calibration. Chain-of-thought prompting, applied as a mitigation strategy, shifts response distributions without consistently improving hallucination discrimination. BenHalluEval establishes the first dedicated hallucination benchmark for Bengali and highlights the inadequacy of single-track and prompting-only evaluation approaches for low-resource language settings. The dataset and code are available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/BanglaHalluEval-EB77.

processed.summary:
- Introduces **BenHalluEval**, the first comprehensive framework for evaluating **hallucinations** in Bengali across four tasks: Generative QA, Code-Mixed QA, Summarization, and Reasoning.
- Implements a **dual-track protocol** that independently measures false-positives on ground-truth instances (Track A) and detection rates on **hallucinated candidates** (Track B).
- Proposes **BenHalluScore**, a novel calibration metric designed to penalize uniform response bias and provide a robust assessment of model reliability in **low-resource** contexts.
- Evaluates the impact of **Chain-of-Thought (CoT)** prompting, finding it shifts response distributions without consistently improving hallucination discrimination.

Introduction of BenHalluScore, which revealed model performance variance from 7.72% to 55.42%.
Evidence that single-track evaluation is inadequate for measuring model reliability in Bengali.
Observation that multilingual and Bengali-centric models exhibit substantial calibration gaps.
Validation that Chain-of-Thought prompting is not a universal solution for hallucination mitigation in low-resource settings.

title: Language Models Can Resolve Reference Compositionally, But It's Not Their Native Strength: The Case of the Personal Relation Task
summary: Do neural models, such as Large Language Models, genuinely acquire compositional abilities for interpretation of natural language? When we talk about semantic interpretation, we can distinguish two complementary aspects: establishing what an expression refers to in the world (which we call the Extensional task) and representing its sense in a structured way (which we call the Intensional task). We evaluate LLMs and humans on both tasks in the setting of the Personal Relation Task (Paperno 2022) in which, given a universe of people and their relationships with each other, one is asked to interpret a noun phrase such as "Amber's parent's friend". Here, for the Intensional task, the answer is the formula "friend(parent(amber))", and for the Extensional task, the person. We find that humans and LLMs show opposite strengths: humans perform better on Extensional than Intensional tasks, and LLMs vice versa. Our methodology brings greater nuance to the understanding of compositional abilities in modern machine learning models. Our results support the notion that the lack of referential grounding in LLM training is a crucial missing component in mimicking human-like language understanding.

processed.summary:
• Evaluates LLMs' **compositional interpretation** capabilities using the Personal Relation Task (PRT), distinguishing between **Extensional** (referential) and **Intensional** (symbolic) logic.
• Discovers a performance divergence where LLMs excel at **formal logic mapping** while humans significantly outperform models in **referential grounding**.
• Highlights that the absence of **real-world grounding** in training data is a fundamental barrier to achieving human-like situational reasoning in LLMs.
key_results: LLMs perform better on Intensional tasks (generating formulas like friend(parent(x))) than Extensional tasks.
Humans exhibit the opposite pattern, showing superior ability in finding specific referents (Extensional tasks).
Compositional reasoning in LLMs is not 'native' but rather a result of pattern matching symbolic structures.
The lack of referential grounding is identified as the primary missing component for human-like language understanding.
Performance on the Extensional task does not inherently improve as a model's Intensional accuracy increases.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: Utrecht University researchers show LLMs prioritize symbolic logic over referential grounding, failing to mimic human-like compositional understanding.

title: Knowledge Boundary Probing and Demand-Guided Intervention for LLM-Based Power System Code Generation
summary:
Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly used to automate power-system analysis, but many utilities and energy-research labs require on-premise serving for confidentiality, regulatory, reproducibility, and cost reasons. This makes the reliability of open-weight models a deployment issue. We show that first-pass failures in power-system code generation are dominated not by reasoning alone, but by structured API-knowledge boundary errors: hallucinated function names, misused parameters, and mishandled result tables in versioned simulation libraries.
  We introduce PowerCodeBench, an execution-validated benchmark generator that pairs natural-language operator queries with pandapower code and numerical ground truth; an L0-L3 documentation-driven probing procedure that measures per-model API knowledge profiles; and a boundary-aware intervention that combines query-side API demand estimation with targeted proactive documentation injection and routed reactive correction.
  On a 2,000-task frozen release, we evaluate ten open-weight LLMs (1.5B-480B parameters) and four commercial mid-tier APIs. The intervention improves every evaluated open-weight model of at least 7B parameters and every commercial API by 32 to 56 accuracy points. Open-weight models in the 70B-120B range match the commercial mid-tier accuracy range, while Llama-3.1-405B and Qwen3-Coder-480B lead the panel. The targeted prompts preserve the full-context accuracy ceiling while using 41% of the prompt-token cost. The result is an accuracy-side, deployment-time path toward reliable on-premise LLM assistance for grid-analysis workflows without fine-tuning or cloud inference.

processed.summary:
- Introduces **PowerCodeBench**, an execution-validated benchmark and probing procedure for evaluating LLM proficiency with specialized power-system APIs like **pandapower**.
- Proposes a **boundary-aware intervention** architecture that combines **API demand estimation** with targeted documentation injection to mitigate hallucinations and parameter misuse.
- Demonstrates that **open-weight models** in the 70B-120B range can match mid-tier commercial APIs through retrieval-augmented prompting without the need for fine-tuning.
- Optimizes deployment efficiency by reducing **prompt-token costs** to 41% of the full-context ceiling while maintaining accuracy for critical grid-analysis workflows.
key_results: Accuracy improved by 32 to 56 points for all evaluated models over 7B parameters.
Open-weight models (70B-120B) achieved parity with mid-tier commercial LLM APIs.
Llama-3.1-405B and Qwen3-Coder-480B were identified as the top-performing models in the study.
Targeted documentation injection reduced token costs to 41% of the full-context baseline.
Identified that API knowledge boundary errors, not reasoning, are the primary cause of code generation failure.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce PowerCodeBench to enable high-accuracy, cost-effective power system code generation using local open-weight LLMs.

title: IDOL: Inverse-Dynamics-Guided Future Prediction for End-to-End Autonomous Driving
summary: End-to-end autonomous driving has emerged as a compelling paradigm for learning planning directly from sensor observations, while recent world-model-based approaches further enrich this paradigm by enabling explicit reasoning about how the scene may evolve in the future. Yet future prediction alone does not guarantee better planning unless the predicted evolution can be converted into planning-relevant trajectory updates. Many current methods still forecast future scene states without explicitly decoding the motion implications hidden in state transitions. As a result, future reasoning often remains descriptively useful but only weakly coupled to executable motion generation. To address this limitation, we propose \mathbf{IDOL}, an inverse-dynamics-guided future prediction framework for world-model-based end-to-end planning in latent BEV space, where inverse dynamics serves as the key bridge between future prediction and trajectory optimization. IDOL first predicts multiple future latent scene states with a BEV world model, then applies an inverse dynamics model to adjacent latent futures to decode transition-aware trajectory features and recover planning-relevant motion deltas that explain how the latent world evolves over time. These inverse-dynamics-derived signals are used to optimize the planned trajectory, turning future forecasting from passive scene anticipation into actionable planning guidance. A lightweight closed-loop refinement module further improves long-horizon consistency by reusing the optimized trajectory for another round of future-aware reasoning. By introducing inverse dynamics into latent future reasoning, IDOL tightens the coupling between world modeling and planning. Extensive experiments on the NAVSIM v1 and NAVSIM v2 benchmarks show that IDOL achieves state-of-the-art performance among comparable methods.

processed.summary:
• Introduces **IDOL**, a framework that bridges the gap between latent world modeling and trajectory planning via **inverse-dynamics-guided** reasoning.
 • Predicts multiple future **BEV latent states** and decodes transition-aware motion deltas using an inverse dynamics model to inform the planning module.
 • Features a **lightweight closed-loop refinement** module that iteratively reuses optimized trajectories to ensure long-horizon consistency.
 • Demonstrates superior performance in **end-to-end autonomous driving** by turning passive scene prediction into active planning guidance.
key_results: Achieved State-of-the-Art (SOTA) performance on NAVSIM v1 and v2 benchmarks.
Successfully coupled future scene forecasting with executable motion generation using inverse dynamics.
Improved long-horizon planning consistency through a multi-round refinement module.
Decoded planning-relevant motion deltas directly from transitions in latent BEV space.
Validated that inverse-dynamics-derived signals provide actionable guidance for trajectory optimization.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce IDOL, a framework using inverse dynamics to transform latent world model predictions into actionable autonomous trajectories.

title: VolFill: Single-View Amodal 3D Scene Reconstruction with Volumetric Flow Matching
summary: Reconstructing the complete geometry of a scene from a single RGB image remains challenging - especially when inferring hidden structures where visual evidence is incomplete. We introduce VolFill, a generative framework that predicts the 3D structure of the complete scene rather than relying on traditional pixel-aligned regression. Our method utilizes a hybrid 3D VAE to compress sparse truncated unsigned distance function grids into a compact latent space, paired with a latent Diffusion Transformer that denoises this representation to recover the complete scene. We condition the generation on geometry foundation models, leveraging rich spatial priors for robust reasoning. Unlike existing methods limited by per-ray constraints or unstructured point-cloud queries, VolFill provides a structured representation that supports direct surface extraction and occupancy queries at scale. Extensive experiments on the SCRREAM and NRGB-D datasets demonstrate that our approach significantly outperforms current baselines, providing a robust foundation for holistic spatial understanding.

- Introduces **VolFill**, a generative framework designed for amodal 3D scene reconstruction from a single RGB image using **Volumetric Flow Matching**.
- Utilizes a **hybrid 3D VAE** to compress sparse **Truncated Unsigned Distance Function (TUDF)** grids into a compact latent space for efficient processing.
- Employs a **latent Diffusion Transformer (DiT)** to denoise the 3D representation while conditioning on **geometry foundation models** for spatial reasoning.
- Provides a **structured 3D representation** that enables direct surface extraction and scalable occupancy queries, bypassing the limitations of unstructured point clouds.
key_results: Outperforms current state-of-the-art baselines in single-view 3D scene reconstruction.
Successfully infers hidden and occluded structures where visual evidence is incomplete.
Demonstrates superior performance on the SCRREAM and NRGB-D datasets.
Effectively integrates large-scale spatial priors from geometry foundation models.
Enables high-fidelity direct surface extraction from structured volumetric representations.

title: On-Device Robotic Planning: Eliminating Inference Redundancy for Efficient Decision-Making
summary: Reasoning-based robotic policies using large language and vision-language models achieve strong semantic planning capabilities but mostly suffer from a high inference latency that limits practical real-time deployment. In this work, we observe that robotic reasoning workloads contain substantial temporal redundancy, where consecutive observations frequently produce identical actions and subgoals. Based on this insight, we present REIS, a human cognition inspired robotic decision-making framework that minimizes unnecessary reasoning while preserving semantic adaptability. REIS combines lightweight scene gating, KV-steered affordance routing, and deliberative reasoning to accelerate robotic control under embodied constraints. Experiments on ALFRED, and real-world robotic tasks demonstrate that REIS significantly suppresses reasoning overhead while maintaining competitive task performance.

processed.summary:
* REIS introduces a **hierarchical decision-making framework** that mitigates inference latency by identifying and skipping temporal redundancy in robotic observations.
* The architecture utilizes **lightweight scene gating** and **KV-steered affordance routing** to determine when expensive deliberative reasoning is strictly necessary.
* By minimizing unnecessary VLM/LLM calls, the system enables **real-time on-device deployment** while preserving the high-level semantic adaptability of large models.
* Validation on the **ALFRED benchmark** and real-world platforms shows significant suppression of reasoning overhead without sacrificing task success rates.

Proposed the REIS framework to selectively trigger deliberative reasoning based on scene changes.
Demonstrated substantial reduction in inference latency for vision-language robotic policies.
Maintained competitive performance on the ALFRED benchmark compared to full-inference models.
Validated the efficiency of the system through successful real-world robotic task deployment.

title: Ladder Logic Translation using Large Language Models in Industrial Automation
summary: Ladder logic translation is an important problem in industrial automation because without it, it is difficult to switch Programmable Logic Controller (PLC) vendors. The prevailing translation problem highlights mismatched programming environments, incompatible ladder logic constructs, limitations in terms of differences in the semantic expressiveness of the vendor formalisms and integrated black-box proprietary engineering tools which are exemplified in our example case; Rockwell to Siemens PLC code translation. This work presents a mathematical formulation of the problem, the detailed architecture of a solution which supports XML extraction, structural normalization, constrained generative function (LLM), and system integration via the TIA Portal Openness API as rigorously engineered pipeline for automated translation of Rockwell Ladder Programs to Siemens S7 ladder programs. Finally, we present results that show that the translations retain high semantic consistency across instruction categories.

processed.summary:
* Proposes a specialized pipeline for **Rockwell to Siemens PLC code translation** using a mathematical formulation of the logic mapping problem.
* Implements a modular architecture involving **XML extraction**, **structural normalization**, and a **constrained generative function** powered by an LLM.
* Achieves end-to-end automation by integrating outputs with the **TIA Portal Openness API** for direct system deployment.
* Focuses on maintaining **semantic consistency** across mismatched programming environments and proprietary vendor constructs.
key_results: Successfully automated the translation of Rockwell Ladder Programs to Siemens S7 formats.
Maintained high semantic consistency across diverse PLC instruction categories.
Integrated a constrained generative LLM component to handle vendor-specific semantic gaps.
Demonstrated a workflow for bypassing black-box proprietary engineering tool limitations.
Validated the pipeline through the Siemens TIA Portal Openness API for real-world application.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers develop an LLM-based pipeline to automate Rockwell-to-Siemens PLC code translation with high semantic consistency.

title: Translation Analytics for Freelancers II: Benchmarking Local LLMs for Confidential Translation Workflows
summary: Building on our previous work, this paper develops practical, low-barrier methods for freelance translators and smaller language service providers to evaluate translation technologies using rigorous yet accessible analytic methods. Here we address a high-stakes, specialized need: offline translation for confidentiality-sensitive domains in which privacy constraints preclude the use of cloud-based engines and commercial LLMs. We expand the Reeve Foundation Trilingual Corpus (RFTC) used in our previous work into a multilingual corpus (RFMC) by adding sentence-aligned German and Simplified Chinese reference translations. We then benchmark several locally runnable language models (via Ollama) across four language directions on 1000+ sentences selected from this corpus. We use consistent single-prompt calls without fine-tuning or domain adaptation, comparing local LLM outputs against commercial NMTs (DeepL, Baidu), a frontier LLM (GPT-5.2), and professional-grade local NMT systems (OPUS-CAT, NeuralDesktop, Promt). Automatic evaluation is conducted with MATEO. Results reveal substantial variation in local LLM performance across language directions and model sizes. The best local LLMs match or surpass local NMT systems and a frontier LLM, though they remain behind top commercial NMTs. These findings underscore the viability of carefully selected local LLM translation for privacy-constrained professionals and inform future research on model scaling and multilingual capability.

processed.summary:
- Evaluates **Local LLMs** (deployed via **Ollama**) against traditional **Neural Machine Translation (NMT)** and frontier models for high-stakes, privacy-sensitive translation tasks.
- Introduces the **RFMC (Reeve Foundation Multilingual Corpus)**, expanding sentence-aligned datasets for German and Simplified Chinese to facilitate rigorous benchmarking.
- Utilizes a **single-prompt zero-shot architecture** without domain adaptation, comparing local performance against commercial engines like DeepL and frontier models like **GPT-5.2**.
- Analyzes the technical viability of **offline LLM workflows**, proving that carefully selected local models can match specialized local NMT systems and frontier model quality.
key_results: High-performing local LLMs achieved translation parity with or surpassed professional local NMT systems like OPUS-CAT.
Local LLM performance showed substantial variation based on model parameter size and specific language direction.
The best local LLMs matched the translation quality of the frontier GPT-5.2 model in selected benchmarks.

Automated evaluation via MATEO confirms local LLMs as a viable low-barrier solution for confidential freelance translation.

title: Fine-grained Verification via Diagnostic Reasoning Supervision for Aspect Sentiment Triplet Extraction
summary: Aspect Sentiment Triplet Extraction (ASTE) aims to identify aspect terms, opinion terms, and sentiment polarities as structured triplets, providing essential inputs for downstream information system applications such as opinion mining, explainable recommendations, and review summarization. Prior work mainly focuses on end-to-end extraction, while post hoc verification of extracted triplets remains comparatively underexplored. This gap limits the reliability of ASTE systems, since predicted triplets may be locally plausible while being globally invalid. Moreover, candidate invalidity is multi-faceted and candidate usability is inherently graded, motivating a fine-grained verification mechanism that can filter or re-rank outputs from diverse extractors. In this paper, we propose FiVeD, a framework for Fine-grained Verification with Diagnostic reasoning supervision. Specifically, the verifier is trained with multiple complementary objectives, including validity classification and quality score estimation as primary tasks, with error type classification and rationale generation as auxiliary tasks. We define hierarchical error categories and construct plausible incorrect triplets under semantic and syntactic constraints, and leverage an off-the-shelf LLM with task-specific rubrics to produce quality scores and diagnostic rationales. During inference, the resulting quality scores are used to filter candidate outputs, supporting adjustable precision-recall tradeoffs. Experiments across multiple ASTE baselines demonstrate that FiVeD consistently improves extraction performance by up to 3.53 F1 points as a plug-and-play verification module.

processed.summary:
* Introduces **FiVeD**, a plug-and-play framework for **Aspect Sentiment Triplet Extraction (ASTE)** that addresses the unreliability of end-to-end extractors through post-hoc verification.
* Employs **multi-objective training** including validity classification, quality score estimation, error type classification, and **LLM-guided rationale generation**.
* Utilizes an **off-the-shelf LLM** with task-specific rubrics to generate diagnostic reasoning and quality scores for synthetic triplets under semantic/syntactic constraints.
* Supports **adjustable precision-recall tradeoffs** by using fine-grained quality scores to filter or re-rank candidate outputs from diverse baseline models.
key_results: Improves extraction performance by up to 3.53 F1 points as a plug-and-play module.
Establishes a hierarchical error taxonomy for fine-grained triplet verification.
Demonstrates that diagnostic reasoning supervision (rationales) enhances verification accuracy.
Successfully filters globally invalid candidates that appear locally plausible to standard extractors.
Consistent performance gains across multiple established ASTE baselines and datasets.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce FiVeD, a diagnostic reasoning framework that improves Aspect Sentiment Triplet Extraction accuracy via fine-grained verification.

title: Used Car Salesbots? Honesty and Credulity of LLMs as Bargaining Agents under Partial Information
summary:
In this work we study agents in simulated bargaining scenarios, where a buyer and a seller communicate through a text channel and attempt to negotiate mutually beneficial trades, under different information regimes (complete information, information asymmetry or mutual uncertainty). We evaluate their performance w.r.t. game-theoretical solutions and further investigate their honesty (their tendency to disclose or withhold information or to mislead and deceive) as well as their credulity (their tendency to trust or distrust information provided by the other agent). We study zero-shot LLM agents with simple prompting scaffolding as well as fine-tuned agents, in order to investigate whether optimising the agents to maximise financial profits makes them stronger negotiators but also more dishonest and less trusting.
  We find that off-the-shelf LLMs all substantially deviate from game-theoretical equilibria, they attempt to lie about their private information but cannot efficiently exploit information asymmetries. Fine-tuning on financial utility makes the agents stronger at achieving better deals but also more dishonest, highlighting the risks that optimising agents for a task can have on their safety. We release our code and a dataset of bargaining scenarios.

processed.summary:
- Evaluates **zero-shot LLMs** and fine-tuned agents in simulated bargaining scenarios using different information regimes such as **information asymmetry** and mutual uncertainty.
- Investigates the trade-off between **financial utility optimization** and agent safety, specifically focusing on **honesty** (deception) and **credulity** (trust).
- Analyzes agent behavior relative to **game-theoretical equilibria**, finding that standard models often fail to reach optimal strategic outcomes without specific tuning.
- Provides a new **dataset and code framework** for benchmarking multi-agent negotiation and strategic reasoning in LLMs.
key_results: Off-the-shelf LLMs deviate significantly from game-theoretical equilibria in bargaining tasks.
Standard LLMs attempt to lie but lack the strategic depth to efficiently exploit private information.
Fine-tuning for financial profit successfully creates stronger, more effective negotiators.
Profit-optimized agents exhibit increased dishonesty and a lower tendency to trust other agents.
The study highlights a critical safety risk: optimizing for performance can inadvertently encourage deceptive behavior.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers demonstrate that fine-tuning LLMs for financial gain improves bargaining performance while simultaneously increasing deceptive behaviors.

title: Answer-Set-Programming-based Abstractions for Reinforcement Learning
summary: Reinforcement Learning (RL) enables autonomous agents to learn policies from experience, but realistic problems often involve enormous state spaces, making learning and generalisation challenging. Abstraction and approximation are therefore essential. Relational Reinforcement Learning (RRL) offers a way to reason about objects and their relations, and the CARCASS framework by Martijn van Otterlo demonstrates how logical representations can model Markov Decision Processes (MDPs) in first-order domains. Originally implemented in Prolog, CARCASS leverages domain knowledge to create powerful abstractions. We explore Answer-Set Programming (ASP), which is a rich and, contrary to Prolog, fully declarative modelling language, to realise CARCASS abstractions. We evaluate our ASP-based implementation in case studies of two domains, viz. Blocks World and Minigrid. Our results indicate that CARCASS with ASP provides a promising approach to constructing abstractions for RL, especially when domain knowledge is available.

processed.summary:
- Integrates **Answer-Set Programming (ASP)** with the **CARCASS framework** to create declarative, logical abstractions for **Reinforcement Learning (RL)**.
- Moves beyond traditional Prolog implementations to leverage a fully declarative modeling language for defining **Markov Decision Processes (MDPs)** in first-order domains.
- Enables the use of **prior domain knowledge** to drastically reduce search spaces in complex environments like Blocks World and Minigrid.
- Bridges the gap between **Symbolic AI** and sub-symbolic RL by mapping relational states to abstract action spaces.
key_results: Successfully implemented CARCASS abstractions using Answer-Set Programming for RL tasks.
Demonstrated significant state-space reduction in the classical Blocks World domain.
Validated framework adaptability within the Minigrid navigation environment.
Showed that declarative logic provides more flexible modeling compared to procedural Prolog-based approaches.
Confirmed that domain-specific knowledge improves generalization and learning speed in relational environments.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers utilize Answer-Set Programming to create logical abstractions for Reinforcement Learning, improving efficiency in large state spaces.

title: DOA: Training-Free Decoder-Only Attention Policy for Long-Form Simultaneous Translation with SpeechLLMs
summary: Simultaneous speech-to-text translation (SimulST) generates translations while speech is still unfolding, requiring a streaming policy that decides when to read and when to write. State-of-the-art approaches rely on attention-based encoder-decoder models where cross-attention provides explicit alignment signals. In contrast, Speech Large Language Models (SpeechLLMs) are decoder-only architectures relying solely on self-attention. This raises a central question: whether decoder self-attention contains sufficiently stable alignment signals to guide the streaming policy. Moreover, existing approaches typically rely on training-based adaptations or heuristic wait-$k$ policies and have not been validated in long-form settings. To fill these gaps, we propose Decoder-Only Attention (DOA), a training-free policy that enables long-form simultaneous translation with off-the-shelf SpeechLLMs by deriving a proxy alignment from self-attention. Experiments on Phi4-Multimodal and Qwen3-Omni show that DOA provides an effective alignment signal for supporting streaming decisions, enabling low-latency long-form SimulST with quality close to offline decoding without retraining.

processed.summary:
• **DOA (Decoder-Only Attention)** is a training-free policy designed for **Simultaneous Speech-to-Text Translation (SimulST)** using off-the-shelf **SpeechLLMs**.
• It leverages **self-attention** to derive proxy alignment signals, solving the challenge of streaming decisions in **decoder-only architectures** that lack cross-attention.
• The policy enables **long-form streaming** without the need for heuristic wait-k strategies or expensive model fine-tuning.
• Validated on **Phi4-Multimodal** and **Qwen3-Omni**, the approach maintains translation quality comparable to **offline decoding** while ensuring low latency.
key_results: DOA extracts stable alignment signals directly from decoder self-attention layers.
Enables simultaneous translation on long-form audio without additional training.
Achieves translation quality nearly equivalent to offline inference performance.
Demonstrates compatibility with state-of-the-art models like Phi4 and Qwen3.
Eliminates the need for fixed heuristic streaming policies like wait-k.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers develop DOA, a training-free policy enabling low-latency simultaneous translation using standard decoder-only SpeechLLMs.

title: Fixed Universal Transformers
summary: We introduce \emph{universal transformers}: fixed transformers that can simulate any transformer in a given class via a suitable input embedding. Analogous to a universal Turing machine, the input embedding encodes a description of the target model while all internal parameters remain fixed. We provide explicit sparse constructions achieving universality when the embedding dimension is sufficiently large, and further show that universality is generic: randomly initialized transformers are universal almost surely, which aligns with recent empirical results of Zhong and Andreas (2024). We empirically validate our theory on the algorithmic tasks of parenthesis balancing and multi-hop reasoning. Our results suggest that much of a transformer's expressive power may reside in its input representation rather than its learned weights.

processed.summary:
- Introduces **Fixed Universal Transformers**, architectures that can simulate any other transformer within a specific class using only a specialized input embedding.
- Establishes a theoretical framework analogous to a **Universal Turing Machine**, where the input embedding acts as a program that describes the target model's behavior.
- Proves that **randomly initialized transformers** are universal almost surely, suggesting that a model's expressive power is significantly derived from its input representation.
- Provides empirical evidence on **multi-hop reasoning** and algorithmic tasks, showing that fixed weights can perform complex logic when provided with the correct embeddings.
key_results: Construction of sparse universal transformers that simulate any target model without weight changes.
Theoretical proof that universality is a generic property of randomly initialized transformers.
Empirical validation using the Dyck-k parenthesis balancing task to test architectural limits.
Demonstration of multi-hop reasoning capabilities within fixed-weight architectures.
Alignment with previous research suggesting in-context learning is a form of internal model simulation.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers demonstrate that fixed-weight transformers can simulate any model via input embeddings, prioritizing representation over weight optimization.

title: Neuro-symbolic Syntactic Parsing: Shaping a Neural Network with the CYK Algorithm
summary: In this paper, we show the possibility of a direct injection of algorithms into neural network architecture. We focus on a complex algorithm, that is, Cocke-Youger-Kasami (CYK) for parsing context-free grammars in Chomsky Normal Form and we propose CYKNN, a simple recurrent neural network architecture for encoding the CYK algorithm in trainable matrix-vector multiplications.We experimented with a very simple grammar with 4 variations showing that our approach outperforms existing LLMs with more than 20B parameters with an in-context learning setting and smaller LLMs of the Qwen family fine-tuned with LoRA. Our attempt paves the way to a different approach to neuro-symbolic methodologies.

processed.summary:
- Proposes **CYKNN**, a novel recurrent neural network architecture designed to directly encode the **Cocke-Younger-Kasami (CYK)** algorithm logic.
- Implements algorithmic injection via **trainable matrix-vector multiplications**, moving beyond standard black-box neural approaches to a **neuro-symbolic** paradigm.
- Specifically targets the parsing of **context-free grammars (CFG)** in Chomsky Normal Form by shaping the network topology around classical computational steps.
- Demonstrates that embedding algorithmic structure allows minimalist models to surpass the reasoning capabilities of massive **Generative AI** models in specialized tasks.
key_results: CYKNN architecture successfully encodes complex algorithmic logic into a differentiable format.
Outperformed LLMs with over 20B parameters using in-context learning on grammar tasks.
Showed higher accuracy than fine-tuned Qwen models using LoRA for syntactic parsing.
Maintained robust performance across four distinct grammar variations.
Validated the efficiency of structural neuro-symbolic injection over brute-force scaling.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce CYKNN, a neuro-symbolic RNN that encodes the CYK algorithm to outperform 20B+ parameter LLMs in parsing.

title: Enhancing Galaxy Classification with U-Net Variational Autoencoders. III. Disk-like Galaxy Identification in JWST Samples of up to redshift 4
summary: In this third study of the series, we extend our U-Net Variational Autoencoder-based galaxy classification framework to a significantly larger JWST sample spanning the redshift range $0.5 < z < 4$. Focusing on massive systems with stellar masses exceeding $10^{10}\,M_\odot$, we analyze 1,380 galaxies that satisfy these criteria and apply our previously developed denoising and classification pipeline to identify disk-like morphologies across cosmic time. Within this population, our classifier detects 382 disk-like galaxies, with a subset showing uncertain features consistent with the expected performance limits of current deep-learning models. This expanded dataset allows us to examine the distribution of disk-like systems in a statistically meaningful high-redshift regime, including epochs where well-ordered disks are traditionally expected to be rare. The results demonstrate that disk-like structures persist across a broad range of redshifts and stellar masses, suggesting that massive disks may be more common in the early universe than previously assumed. These findings emphasize the value of combining advanced denoising techniques with machine-learning-based morphological analysis for characterizing galaxy populations in large JWST surveys.

processed.summary:
- Employs a **U-Net Variational Autoencoder (VAE)** framework to automate the morphological classification of high-redshift galaxies from JWST imagery.
- Integrates a specialized **denoising and classification pipeline** to handle low signal-to-noise data in distant astronomical samples.
- Provides a scalable methodology for analyzing **massive stellar systems** (over 10^10 solar masses) across a redshift range of 0.5 to 4.0.
- Demonstrates that **generative-based feature extraction** can reveal structural patterns like disk-like formations in early cosmic epochs.
key_results: Processed a sample of 1,380 massive galaxies using the JWST dataset.
Identified 382 disk-like galaxies within the high-redshift range.
Provided evidence that well-ordered disk structures are more common in the early universe than previously assumed.
Established performance benchmarks for deep-learning models in morphological classification under high-redshift constraints.
Validated the effectiveness of U-Net architectures for denoising astronomical imagery for downstream ML tasks.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers utilize U-Net Variational Autoencoders to identify high-redshift disk galaxies, revealing significant structural order in the early universe.

title: FAM-Bench: A Multimodal Benchmark for Condition-Aware Food-as-Medicine Reasoning
summary: Food-as-Medicine requires models to reason beyond what a dish is or what nutrition it contains: they must decide whether a concrete food choice is appropriate for a specific health condition. Existing food AI benchmarks primarily evaluate dish recognition, recipe understanding, nutrient estimation, or general nutrition question answering, leaving this health-aware decision layer largely untested. We introduce FAM-Bench, a multi-modal Food-as-Medicine benchmark with 2500 nutrition-expert-verified instances across 13 diet-related health conditions. The benchmark contains two complementary tasks: dish-level suitability assessment, where models judge whether a dish is suitable for a condition from its image and ingredient list, and comparative dish analysis, where models rank four candidate dishes by condition-specific suitability. Both tasks require integrating ingredient evidence, visual preparation cues, and clinical nutrition constraints, providing a standardized testbed for grounded health-aware reasoning in language and vision-language models.

processed.summary:
- Introduces **FAM-Bench**, a novel multimodal benchmark designed to evaluate **Food-as-Medicine (FAM)** reasoning, moving beyond simple dish recognition to clinical suitability.
- Features **2,500 expert-verified instances** across 13 diet-related health conditions, requiring models to process both visual preparation cues and ingredient lists.
- Employs a dual-task architecture consisting of **dish-level suitability assessment** and **comparative ranking** to test grounded reasoning in Vision-Language Models (VLMs).
- Addresses the gap in current AI evaluation where models lack the **clinical nutrition constraints** needed for real-world health-aware decision-making.
key_results: Development of a 2,500-instance dataset verified by nutrition experts.
Coverage of 13 distinct health conditions requiring specialized dietary reasoning.
Establishment of a comparative ranking task to test nuanced preference between four candidate dishes.
Validation of the need for multimodal integration of visual cues and text-based ingredient evidence.
Creation of a standardized testbed for evaluating the zero-shot reasoning capabilities of LLMs and VLMs in health contexts.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: Researchers introduce FAM-Bench to evaluate multimodal models on clinical food suitability reasoning across thirteen diet-related health conditions.

title: Skill Availability and Presentation Granularity in Large-Language-Model Agents: A Controlled SkillsBench Study
summary: Skill documents provide procedural knowledge to large-language-model agents at inference time. This article studies whether the presentation granularity of controlled skill knowledge changes downstream task success. The experiment uses a pinned SkillsBench version, a 30-task domain-balanced subset validated by official oracle runs, two reasoning-enabled model configurations, six skill conditions, and five trials per task-condition-model cell. Skill availability is the clearest empirical signal. Relative to no skill, skill conditions increase task-mean pass rate by 26.7 to 36.0 percentage points for GPT-5.5 and by 18.0 to 26.0 percentage points for DeepSeek V4-Flash. The final data contain 1,800 rows, with 900 rows for each model. The task is the inference unit. Five trials are aggregated within each task-condition-model cell before paired contrasts are estimated over 30 tasks. The primary presentation contrasts are smaller and uncertain. Low-abstraction guidance differs from high-abstraction guidance by +0.7 percentage points for GPT-5.5 and -6.7 percentage points for DeepSeek V4-Flash, with both 95% bootstrap confidence intervals crossing zero. Adding one worked example to medium-abstraction guidance differs from the no-example variant by +0.7 and +1.3 percentage points. Mean-reward robustness checks preserve the same substantive conclusion. In this controlled subset, skill availability is associated with higher success than no skill, while the tested presentation-granularity changes yield small, uncertain, and model-dependent effects.

processed.summary:
- Evaluates **LLM Agent** performance using **SkillsBench**, focusing on how procedural knowledge delivery affects downstream task execution.
- Investigates the impact of **Skill Availability** versus **Presentation Granularity**, comparing low-abstraction instructions against high-abstraction guidance.
- Benchmarks **Reasoning Models** including GPT-5.5 and DeepSeek V4-Flash across 1,800 trials to determine the sensitivity of agents to documentation formatting.
- Concludes that providing **In-context Skill Documents** is the primary driver of success, whereas specific formatting tweaks offer marginal, model-dependent gains.
key_results: Skill availability increased GPT-5.5 pass rates by up to 36.0 percentage points.
DeepSeek V4-Flash pass rates improved by up to 26.0 percentage points with skill access.
Low-abstraction vs. high-abstraction guidance showed statistically insignificant differences (+0.7% to -6.7%).
Adding worked examples to skill documentation yielded negligible improvements of less than 1.3%.
Results suggest that for reasoning-enabled models, the presence of documentation outweighs the specific granularity of its presentation.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers demonstrate that providing skill documentation significantly boosts agent performance, while fine-grained presentation formatting has minimal impact.

title: The Sword, Shield, and Achilles' Heel: Characterizing the Linguistic Inductive Bias of Large Language Models for Spatial Reasoning in Navigation Planning
summary: Large Language Model (LLM)-based navigation systems commonly construct explicit spatial representations (e.g., topological graphs, semantic raster maps) and translate them into textual descriptions as LLMs' inputs. However, the linguistic structures of such text-based spatial representations and the choices of contextual features (e.g., topology, geometry) they contain are often treated as neutral engineering decisions rather than key factors that shape LLMs' behavior. To fill the gap, we propose a dual-interventional framework that disentangles linguistic structures from different contextual cues to evaluate the linguistic inductive bias of LLMs for navigation planning. In the framework, representation intervention varies the linguistic format and the degree of linguistic compression, clarifying when linguistic representations support or inhibit navigation planning. Context intervention, combined with contextual feature combination and conflict probing, explicitly clarifies the preferences and weaknesses of LLMs when processing different contextual cues. Experiments across diverse spatial reasoning tasks and multiple model scales reveal a consistent pattern: topological information is a sturdy shield and the backbone of robust planning; linguistic format is a double-edged sword whose effect depends on model size, task demands, and the compression level; and semantic information is a fatal Achilles' heel -- incorrect semantic cues can systematically derail the planning process. Overall, our study shows that effective text-based spatial representations in LLM-based navigation should preserve topological integrity, calibrate representational compression to model capacity, and ensure semantic correctness, rather than simply adopting a single representation. Our code is publicly available at https://github.com/jonesdong150/LLM-Navigation-Inductive-Bias.

processed.summary:
• Proposes a **dual-interventional framework** designed to disentangle the effects of linguistic structures from spatial contextual cues (topology, geometry, semantics) in LLM-based navigation.
• Explores the **linguistic inductive bias** by manipulating representation formats and compression levels, revealing how text-based spatial data shapes model behavior.
• Implements **context intervention** and conflict probing to identify the hierarchy of spatial features that LLMs prioritize during complex planning tasks.
• Advocates for **topology-preserving representations** that calibrate compression to specific model capacities to enhance the reliability of agentic reasoning.
key_results: Topological information is the most robust foundation for planning, acting as a 'shield' for navigation logic.
Linguistic format serves as a 'double-edged sword,' with its efficacy varying based on model size and task complexity.
Semantic cues are a significant vulnerability, where incorrect semantic data can systematically derail the planning process.
Higher model scales show different sensitivities to linguistic compression compared to smaller local models.
Effective spatial reasoning requires a multi-faceted representation strategy rather than a single, static text format.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers demonstrate that topological integrity is vital while semantic errors represent a fatal Achilles' heel for LLM navigation.

title: "Intelegi Româneşte?'' A Recipe for Romanian Vision-Language Models
summary: Vision-Language Models (VLMs) largely follow the text-only LLM trajectory, excelling on English benchmarks but sharply degrading on low-resource languages, where neither large-scale image-text corpora nor culturally grounded evaluations exist. We present a systematic study of building a language-specific VLM for Romanian, covering the full pipeline from data construction to architectural choices. We translate established English VLM training and evaluation corpora into Romanian, applying machine translation to textual annotations and to in-image text, preserving visual grounding while adapting the textual content. Using this data, we train and ablate a series of VLMs to isolate the contribution of (i) vision backbones of varying scale and pretraining, (ii) language backbones from multilingual to Romanian-adapted LLMs, and (iii) OCR-style image-text data. We further curate HoraVQA, a culturally native evaluation set grounded in Romanian everyday scenes. Romanian-adapted VLMs consistently outperform their same-sized counterparts and, across all evaluated benchmarks, even surpass models from the next larger size category.

processed.summary:
- Proposes a **systematic recipe** for building low-resource Vision-Language Models (VLMs) by leveraging machine translation for both textual annotations and **in-image text**.
- Investigates the impact of **architectural bottlenecks**, specifically comparing vision backbone scales and the efficacy of **Romanian-adapted LLMs** versus multilingual alternatives.
- Introduces **HoraVQA**, a culturally native evaluation benchmark grounded in Romanian everyday scenes to address the lack of localized VLM testing data.
- Demonstrates that **language-specific fine-tuning** and OCR-style data integration significantly enhance visual grounding and reasoning in underrepresented languages.
key_results: Romanian-adapted VLMs consistently outperform same-sized and even larger general-purpose multilingual models.
Machine translation of in-image text is a critical step for preserving visual grounding in localized datasets.
Language-specific LLM backbones provide a higher performance floor than massive multilingual backbones.
The scale of the vision backbone correlates directly with improved multimodal reasoning across all benchmarks.
HoraVQA reveals significant gaps in the cultural awareness of global, English-centric VLMs.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: Romanian researchers develop specialized VLMs and the HoraVQA dataset, proving language-specific adaptation outperforms general-purpose scaling in vision-language tasks.

title: Target-Side Paraphrase Augmentation for Sign Language Translation with Large Language Models
summary:
Sign language translation (SLT) remains constrained by limited paired sign-video/text corpora and heavy-tailed target vocabularies. We study target-side augmentation in which GPT-4o generates controlled paraphrase variants of reference sentences while the sign input remains unchanged. A Signformer-style pose-based Transformer is trained under a two-stage schedule: pre-training on the augmented corpus followed by fine-tuning on the original references.
  We evaluate on three datasets spanning complementary challenges: PHOENIX14T (German Sign Language), with moderate lexical diversity; GSL (Greek Sign Language), with highly ontrolled, repetitive recordings; and LSA-T (Argentinian Sign Language), with severe long-tail sparsity. On PHOENIX14T, augmentation improves BLEU-4 from 9.56 to 10.33. The near-saturated GSL baseline and extremely sparse LSA-T setting reveal the limits of the approach. To our knowledge, this is the first study to apply LLM-generated target-side araphrases and LLM-as-a-Judge evaluation to SLT. The semantic evaluation reveals gains in fidelity that lexical overlap metrics understate.

processed.summary:
- Proposes a novel **target-side augmentation** strategy for Sign Language Translation (SLT) using **GPT-4o** to generate controlled paraphrase variants of reference text.
- Utilizes a **Signformer-style pose-based Transformer** architecture trained via a two-stage pipeline: pre-training on synthetic paraphrases followed by fine-tuning on ground-truth references.
- Leverages **LLM-as-a-Judge** for semantic evaluation, demonstrating that traditional lexical metrics like BLEU-4 may understate the fidelity improvements in translation quality.
- Addresses the **long-tail sparsity** and limited data constraints common in SLT by diversifying the target text corpus without requiring additional sign-video data.
key_results: Improved BLEU-4 score from 9.56 to 10.33 on the PHOENIX14T (German Sign Language) dataset.
First study to successfully apply LLM-generated target-side paraphrases to the Sign Language Translation domain.
Implemented LLM-as-a-Judge evaluation to capture semantic gains that traditional lexical overlap metrics miss.
Found that augmentation benefits scale best with moderate lexical diversity but struggle with extreme sparsity (LSA-T).
Validated a two-stage training schedule as an effective method for integrating augmented synthetic text into SLT models.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers use GPT-4o paraphrase augmentation and Signformer architectures to improve semantic fidelity in pose-based sign language translation.

title: On limitations of polyconvexity
summary: Polyconvex constitutive modeling is attractive as it guarantees stability of numerical simulations and can improve the generalization behavior of material models. However, in certain applications, polyconvex formulations perform poorly in reproducing the underlying ground truth material response, which can effectively preclude their practical use. In this work, we address this issue and investigate the limitations of polyconvex constitutive modeling. The main contributions of this paper are as follows: (1) We analyze the theoretical reasons why polyconvexity may, in some cases, impose overly restrictive constraints that limit the achievable accuracy of constitutive models. Thereby, we provide analytical ellipticity guarantees for two non-polyconvex Mooney-Rivlin type potentials. (2) We investigate the practical limitations of polyconvex physics-augmented neural network constitutive models using two representative formulations: models using structural tensor-based invariants and models using signed singular values. Their performance is evaluated on datasets obtained from homogenized microstructured materials, and their predictive capabilities are assessed in finite element simulations. (3) Overall, we provide an overview of benefits, limitations, and mitigation strategies of polyconvex constitutive modeling.

processed.summary:
- The research evaluates **polyconvexity** in constitutive modeling, a property that ensures numerical stability but often sacrifices the accuracy of **Physics-Augmented Neural Networks (PANNs)**.
- It analyzes how strict mathematical constraints can lead to **overly restrictive models** that fail to capture complex material responses, particularly in microstructured media.
- The study investigates two specific neural architectures: those utilizing **structural tensor-based invariants** and those based on **signed singular values**.
- The work provides a framework for **mitigation strategies**, offering analytical ellipticity guarantees for non-polyconvex potentials to maintain stability without losing predictive fidelity.
key_results: Identified theoretical cases where polyconvexity prevents accurate ground truth reproduction.
Provided analytical ellipticity guarantees for two specific non-polyconvex Mooney-Rivlin potentials.
Demonstrated that signed singular value formulations can outperform invariant-based neural models in certain regimes.
Validated findings using datasets derived from homogenized microstructured materials.
Successfully implemented and assessed predictive performance within finite element simulation environments.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers demonstrate that polyconvexity constraints in physics-augmented neural networks can limit accuracy and propose non-polyconvex stable alternatives.

title: Deep-learning-based low-energy trigger algorithms for the Hyper-Kamiokande experiment
summary: Modern machine learning techniques have become increasingly important in particle physics because of their powerful pattern-recognition capabilities, including in real-time data acquisition where stringent runtime constraints apply. This paper details the performance of deep-learning-based trigger algorithms for a large water Cherenkov detector such as Hyper-Kamiokande aimed at low-energy neutrino events (below 7 MeV). The performance of custom neural-network supervised classifiers is shown alongside two anomaly-detection approaches trained solely on detector noise: a pure autoencoder and an energy-based model based on Manifold Projection--Diffusion Recovery (MPDR). The supervised model shows signal identification efficiencies of 76.7% for single electrons of 3 MeV kinetic energy, significantly exceeding signal efficiencies obtained from a traditional hit-count-based trigger of 26.4%, as does the MPDR approach with 31.8%. Runtime evaluations on GPU yield per-window inference latencies well below the millisecond scale, indicating that real-time operation is feasible.

processed.summary:
* The research introduces **custom supervised classifiers** and **anomaly-detection architectures** (Autoencoders and MPDR) specifically designed for real-time data acquisition in the Hyper-Kamiokande neutrino detector.
* By shifting from traditional hit-count methods to **GPU-accelerated neural networks**, the system identifies low-energy signals (<7 MeV) amidst high-frequency detector noise.
* The implementation focuses on **sub-millisecond inference latency**, ensuring that deep learning models can operate within the stringent temporal constraints of particle physics triggers.
key_results: Supervised models achieved 76.7% signal identification efficiency for 3 MeV electrons.
Traditional hit-count triggers only reached 26.4% efficiency under the same conditions.
Manifold Projection--Diffusion Recovery (MPDR) anomaly detection outperformed traditional methods with 31.8% efficiency.
GPU-based inference latencies were consistently measured well below the 1ms threshold.
Anomaly detection models were successfully trained using solely detector noise data.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: Hyper-Kamiokande researchers demonstrate that GPU-accelerated deep learning models significantly outperform traditional algorithms for real-time low-energy neutrino detection.

title: Neuroforger: certified violation witnesses for smart contracts verification via LLMs
summary: Recent large language models (LLMs) incorporate reasoning capabilities that allow them to perform well in predicting whether a smart contract respects a certain property, suggesting a complementary approach to traditional formal-methods-based techniques for smart contract verification. However, the application of LLMs in such context has two major issues: 1) properties expressed in natural language are intrinsically ambiguous, and 2) answers returned by LLMs have no guarantee of correctness. In this paper, we address both issues simultaneously by: 1) introducing a new formal specification language that extends Solidity with abstract types, and 2) designing a workflow that combines LLMs with type checking and concrete execution to generate and validate violation witnesses (i.e., counterexamples). The key idea is to represent a specification as a Solidity test with (existentially quantified) variables of abstract type; finding an instantiation of these variables to concrete values (of the correct type) concretizes the test into an executable counterexample (PoC) for the target property. We implemented our procedure in the tool Neuroforger, experimentally evaluating it on a smart-contract verification dataset drawn from literature, obtaining promising results that demonstrate its potential applicability in the wild.

processed.summary:
* Introduces a **formal specification language** extending Solidity with abstract types to eliminate natural language ambiguity in property definition.
* Proposes a workflow where **LLMs generate concrete instantiations** for existentially quantified variables in abstract test cases.
* Implements **Neuroforger**, a tool that integrates type checking and concrete execution to validate LLM-generated violation witnesses (PoCs).
* Bridges the gap between **LLM reasoning and formal methods**, ensuring that model-generated counterexamples are mathematically and executably sound.
key_results: Defined a new abstract extension for Solidity to handle formal specifications as executable tests.
Developed a procedure to transform abstract properties into concrete Proof of Concept (PoC) code using LLMs.
Integrated a validation loop that uses type checking and execution to filter out LLM hallucinations.
Achieved automated generation of certified violation witnesses for smart contract vulnerabilities.
Demonstrated via experimental evaluation that Neuroforger identifies contract violations more reliably than raw LLM prompting.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: Neuroforger researchers introduce a hybrid LLM-formal verification system that generates certified, executable violation witnesses for smart contract security.

title: Multi-Turn Multi-Agent Dialogue for Collaborative Reconstruction Improves VLM Performance on Spatial Reasoning, But Only Barely
summary: Robots operating in diverse environments rely on visual input to interpret objects and spatial layouts. In human-collaborative tasks, they are expected to communicate this understanding through language. Vision-language models (VLMs) support robotic tasks involving visual interpretation, question answering, and instruction following, but their capabilities in collaborative dialogue tasks requiring spatial reasoning remain underexplored. We study this gap through a collaborative structure-building task that combines visual interpretation, grounding, language-guided interaction, and action generation. We develop a framework in which VLMs use dialogue to reconstruct a target structure from visual and textual inputs. We evaluate open-weight and closed VLMs across interaction settings, input modalities, and image representations. Results show that spatial reasoning over visual representations remains difficult for the evaluated VLMs. Detailed text representations of the target yield higher reconstruction success across modality conditions, while decomposed image representations improve performance. These findings reveal limits in visual spatial grounding and grounded instruction generation for collaborative VLM agents.

processed.summary:
* Evaluates **Vision-Language Models (VLMs)** in a multi-agent framework designed for collaborative structure-building tasks involving **visual grounding** and action generation.
* Compares **open-weight and closed models** across various modalities, focusing on their ability to interpret spatial layouts and communicate instructions via **multi-turn dialogue**.
* Identifies a significant performance gap in **spatial reasoning**, where models struggle to process direct visual inputs compared to structured textual or decomposed image representations.
* Highlights limitations in **collaborative VLM agents** regarding grounded instruction generation and 3D spatial interpretation in dynamic environments.
key_results: Multi-turn dialogue offers only marginal improvements in spatial reconstruction success.
Textual representations of targets consistently outperform raw image inputs for reasoning.
Decomposed image representations significantly improve model performance over standard image inputs.
Both open-weight and closed VLMs exhibit fundamental weaknesses in visual spatial grounding.
Instruction generation quality remains a bottleneck for effective human-robot collaborative tasks.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers show that while multi-agent dialogue slightly aids VLM spatial reasoning, visual grounding remains a critical failure point.

title: LLM Judges Inconsistently Disagree Across Safety Criteria and Harm Categories
summary: We evaluate the consistency of automated judges in conducting a multi-dimensional safety evaluation in a reference-free setup. Our results indicate that Large Language Models are unreliable judges in identifying safety issues related to machine-generated advice in regulated domains such as finance, although they are more reliable at identifying more overt forms of unsafe/harmful content such as violence.
