The degree of inconsistency in a model's judgments can vary significantly by the chosen safety criteria and can be impacted by the language of the content and its linguistic style as well. Finally, there is high disagreement among different judges for the same output, across domains, safety criteria, and languages. These findings provide new insights on the practice of using LLMs as evaluators and offer several recommendations for practitioners on how to use automated judges in practical scenarios.

processed.summary:
- The study evaluates the **multi-dimensional safety consistency** of LLMs acting as automated judges in a reference-free evaluation framework.
- Researchers found that LLMs exhibit high **judgmental volatility** when assessing regulated domains like finance compared to overt harms like physical violence.
- The paper highlights that **linguistic style** and content language significantly degrade the reliability of **LLM-as-a-judge** architectures.
- Findings suggest that relying on a single model for **automated safety benchmarking** is insufficient due to high inter-model disagreement across diverse criteria.
key_results: LLM judges are highly unreliable at identifying safety issues in regulated domains like finance.
Overt harmful content, such as violence, is identified with significantly higher consistency.
Judgment consistency varies drastically based on the specific safety criteria selected.
Linguistic style and the language of the prompt act as major confounding factors in evaluation.
High rates of disagreement persist among different LLM judges when evaluating the same output.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv Research reveals significant inconsistencies in LLM-based safety evaluations, particularly within regulated domains and across different linguistic styles.

title: Unlocking Fine-Grained Translation Quality Estimation in LRMs through Synergistically Evolving Implicit and Explicit Reasoning
summary: Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) still struggle with fine-grained translation quality estimation (QE), even with long reasoning chains. We argue that LRMs already possess strong multilingual capabilities, while the core challenge stems from the intrinsic difficulty of learning the fine-grained QE task. In this paper, we propose RIEQE (Reasoning both Implicitly and Explicitly for QE), a simple two-stage training framework that enables the co-evolution of implicit (layer-wise) and explicit (token-wise) reasoning capabilities. To make implicit reasoning feasible, we first decompose the complex QE task into straightforward subtasks. Based on this, our two-stage approach applies: (1) NonThinking-SFT, Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT) without reasoning chains to directly boost the model's implicit reasoning tendency and capability; and (2) Thinking-RLVR, standard Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Reward (RLVR) to subsequently strengthen explicit reasoning. Results demonstrate that implicit and explicit reasoning synergistically co-evolve under our framework. On the WMT test sets, RIEQE based on Qwen3-4B-Thinking-2507 surpasses all baselines in explicit reasoning performance, while its implicit reasoning capability is also comparable to the best current encoder-based models. We further provide evidence for the synergistic collaboration between implicit and explicit reasoning, showing how they mutually benefit each other.

processed.summary:
• Introduces **RIEQE**, a novel two-stage training framework designed to bridge the gap between Large Reasoning Models (LRMs) and fine-grained translation quality estimation (QE).
 • Employs a **two-stage training strategy**: NonThinking-SFT for developing implicit, layer-wise reasoning and Thinking-RLVR (Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Reward) for structured explicit reasoning.
 • Utilizes **task decomposition** to break down complex QE evaluation into simpler subtasks, allowing the model to internalize fine-grained error detection more effectively.
 • Demonstrates a **synergistic co-evolution** where improving the model's internal latent reasoning directly enhances its ability to produce accurate, human-readable reasoning chains.
key_results: RIEQE based on Qwen3-4B-Thinking outperforms all existing baselines in explicit translation reasoning performance.
The framework's implicit reasoning capability reaches parity with state-of-the-art specialized encoder-based models.
Proves that explicit reasoning (CoT) and implicit reasoning (internalized logic) mutually benefit each other during the RLVR phase.
Smaller 4B parameter models using RIEQE can surpass significantly larger general-purpose LLMs in fine-grained QE tasks.
Validation on WMT test sets confirms high accuracy in identifying specific translation errors through structured reasoning.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: Alibaba Qwen Team introduces RIEQE to master translation quality estimation by synergistically training implicit and explicit model reasoning.

title: DynaTree: Dynamic Agentic Retrieval Tree for Time-Sensitive News Retrieval
summary: Agentic Retrieval-Augmented Generation improves retrieval by integrating planning, tool use, and iterative reasoning, but existing agentic RAG methods often couple semantic expansion with retrieval decisions in short-horizon inference loops, leading to high inference cost and limited suitability for time-sensitive news retrieval. We propose DynaTree, a two-stage framework for efficient and adaptive news retrieval. In the offline stage, DynaTree uses coordinated agents to construct a reusable retrieval tree that materializes the semantic space of a query topic. In the online stage, DynaTree performs lightweight daily subtree selection over a time-localized evaluation proxy, without further agentic reasoning, tree modification, or retraining. Experiments on a multi-day Syft news benchmark and multiple BEIR datasets show that DynaTree achieves strong recall and ranking performance, consistently outperforming standard RAG and prior agentic baselines. We further deploy DynaTree in the Syft production system and evaluate it through online A/B testing from Jan. 28 to Feb. 6, 2026. The dynamically adapted variant improves survival rate from 0.32-0.53 to 0.59-0.73 over a fixed offline-selected subtree and outperforms existing production recallers on every evaluation day. These results show that persistent, structure-aware semantic expansion can translate offline agentic reasoning into practical improvements in coverage, freshness, and relevance for real-world news retrieval.

processed.summary:
- Introduces **DynaTree**, a two-stage agentic RAG framework designed to decouple computationally expensive semantic reasoning from high-frequency news retrieval.
- Employs an **offline stage** where coordinated agents build a persistent **Retrieval Tree** to map out the semantic space of query topics without per-query latency overhead.
- Utilizes a **lightweight online stage** for daily subtree selection via a time-localized evaluation proxy, ensuring high relevance to fresh news cycles without retraining.
- Bridges the gap between **Agentic RAG** and production-scale inference by materializing offline reasoning into a reusable, structure-aware retrieval artifact.
key_results: Outperformed standard RAG and existing agentic baselines on both Syft and BEIR benchmarks.
Improved retrieval survival rates from 0.32-0.53 up to 0.59-0.73 in production A/B testing.
Demonstrated consistent daily superiority over legacy production recallers during a 10-day evaluation.
Reduced online inference costs by eliminating the need for iterative agentic reasoning at query time.
Achieved significant gains in recall and ranking for time-sensitive, rapidly changing news datasets.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: Syft Research introduces DynaTree to decouple agentic semantic expansion from online retrieval, boosting news retrieval freshness and efficiency.

title: Softsign: Smooth Sign in Your Optimizer For Better Parameter Heterogeneity Handling
summary: Sign-based and LMO-inspired optimizers have recently attracted substantial attention in deep learning due to their strong performance and low memory footprint. However, their fixed-magnitude updates can hurt terminal convergence: they decouple update mechanisms from gradient magnitudes and fail to account for parameter heterogeneity, often leading to oscillation rather than convergence. We propose SoftSignum, a smooth relaxation of sign-based optimization that replaces the hard sign map with a temperature-controlled soft-sign transformation, enabling a parameter-wise transition from sign-like updates to magnitude-sensitive SGD-like steps. We complement it with an adaptive quantile-based temperature schedule and extend the same principle to matrix-valued optimizers, obtaining SoftMuon. We also develop a generalized geometry-relaxation framework based on strongly convex regularizers and Fenchel conjugates, proving convergence in stochastic non-convex setting. Experiments on diverse deep learning tasks, including LLM pretraining, show that SoftSignum and SoftMuon consistently improve over their hard sign-based counterparts and standard AdamW.

processed.summary:
• The paper introduces **SoftSignum** and **SoftMuon**, which replace hard sign maps with **temperature-controlled soft-sign transformations** to bridge the gap between sign-based and SGD-like updates.
• It establishes a **generalized geometry-relaxation framework** using strongly convex regularizers and Fenchel conjugates, providing theoretical convergence proofs in stochastic non-convex settings.
• The architecture utilizes an **adaptive quantile-based temperature schedule**, allowing the optimizer to handle **parameter heterogeneity** more effectively than fixed-magnitude methods.
• Impact includes superior terminal convergence and performance in **LLM pretraining** compared to standard AdamW and traditional sign-based optimizers.
key_results: SoftSignum and SoftMuon consistently outperform AdamW and hard sign-based counterparts in LLM pretraining benchmarks.
The proposed smooth relaxation reduces terminal oscillations common in fixed-magnitude sign-based optimizers.
Introduction of an adaptive quantile-based schedule effectively manages the transition from sign-like to magnitude-sensitive updates.
Successfully extended sign-relaxation principles to matrix-valued optimization via the SoftMuon variant.
Provides a formal theoretical framework for convergence in non-convex settings using strongly convex regularization.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: HKUST researchers introduce SoftSignum and SoftMuon to improve LLM pretraining by relaxing hard sign-based optimization for better convergence.
processed.lead_institution: Hong Kong University of Science and Technology (HKUST)

title: HypoAgent: An Agentic Framework for Interactive Abductive Hypothesis Generation over Knowledge Graphs
summary: Abductive reasoning over knowledge graphs aims to generate logical hypotheses that explain observed entities or facts. Existing controllable hypothesis generation methods allow users to guide this process with explicit conditions, but they remain limited in interactive settings: they struggle to ground evolving natural-language intents across multi-turn dialogues and provide little fine-grained diagnosis when generated hypotheses fail. To address these limitations, we propose HypoAgent, an Agentic framework for interactive abductive Hypothesis Generation over knowledge graphs. HypoAgent integrates three agents: an Intent Recognition Agent that grounds user utterances and dialogue history into executable KG conditions, a Hypothesis Generation Agent that performs controllable hypothesis generation according to the extracted user intention, and a Root Cause Analysis Agent that diagnoses unreliable hypothesis fragments and leverages KG neighborhood probing to identify supported refinements. Experiments on commonsense and biomedical domain-specific knowledge graphs demonstrate that HypoAgent achieves state-of-the-art semantic similarity under single-turn, multi-turn, and unconditional settings. Our code is available at https://github.com/HKUST-KnowComp/HypoAgent.

processed.summary:
- Introduces **HypoAgent**, a multi-agent framework designed to perform **abductive reasoning** over knowledge graphs through interactive natural language dialogues.
- Employs a three-agent architecture: an **Intent Recognition Agent** for intent grounding, a **Hypothesis Generation Agent** for controllable generation, and a **Root Cause Analysis Agent** for diagnostic refinement.
- Utilizes **Knowledge Graph (KG) neighborhood probing** to identify and fix unreliable hypothesis fragments, significantly improving the precision of logical explanations.
- Bridges the gap between evolving natural language user intents and structured symbolic reasoning for **commonsense and biomedical** domains.
key_results: Achieved state-of-the-art semantic similarity across single-turn and multi-turn settings.
Demonstrated superior performance on commonsense and biomedical domain-specific knowledge graphs.
Effectively grounded natural language dialogue history into executable KG conditions.
Improved hypothesis reliability through automated fine-grained diagnosis of failing fragments.
Maintained high performance even in unconditional (non-interactive) generation scenarios.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: HKUST researchers introduce HypoAgent, a multi-agent framework that improves knowledge graph hypothesis generation through interactive dialogue and root-cause diagnosis.

title: The Latin Substrate: How Language Models Represent and Mediate Script Choice
summary:
Many languages are written in multiple scripts, requiring large language models (LLMs) to generate equivalent linguistic content in distinct orthographic forms. While prior work suggests that LLMs route information through shared latent representations, how they internally mediate script variation remains poorly understood.
  We study this question by first examining per-layer output distributions with the logit lens, which reveals consistent latent romanization during transliteration, and then through representational and mechanistic analyses of script generation. At the representational level, we show that scripts of the same language become increasingly separable across layers and that a simple linear steering direction can flip a model's output script while largely maintaining semantic content. The vector generalizes asymmetrically to writing systems unseen during construction, flipping non-Latin output to Latin reliably, but mapping Latin output into varied non-Latin scripts. At the mechanistic level, we localize a small set of late-layer attention heads that causally mediate script choice. These heads transfer across unrelated languages and writing systems, suggesting that script routing is implemented by language-agnostic components. Across both analyses, we observe a consistent directional asymmetry: non-Latin output is produced by a compact, identifiable gate, while Latin-script output emerges from diffuse contributions across the network. Collectively, our findings hint that LLMs organize script variation around shared latent representations while exhibiting a privileged substrate toward Latin script.

processed.summary:
- Investigates how LLMs manage **multilingual script variation** through the lens of mechanistic interpretability and **logit lens** analysis.
- Identifies a **latent romanization** phenomenon where the model internally represents tokens in Latin script before converting to the target orthography.
- Discovers that script routing is controlled by a small set of **late-layer attention heads** that are language-agnostic and functionally transferable.
- Reveals a **structural asymmetry** in model architecture where Latin acts as a privileged substrate, requiring less specialized gating than non-Latin scripts.
key_results: Models exhibit consistent internal romanization during transliteration tasks.
A linear steering direction can flip output scripts while preserving the original semantic meaning.
Script-specific routing is localized to specific 'script heads' in the final layers of the network.
Steering vectors generalize asymmetrically, reliably shifting non-Latin to Latin but not vice versa.
The Latin script is treated as a default representation, while non-Latin scripts use identifiable gating mechanisms.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers demonstrate that LLMs use a Latin-centric internal representation to mediate script variation across diverse languages.

title: Dreaming Of Others: Latent Teammate Modeling In World Models For Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning
summary: In cooperative multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL), agents must coordinate with partners whose internal policies and intentions are not directly observable. While world models such as Dreamer have demonstrated strong generalization and sample efficiency in single-agent settings, their application to MARL remains limited by an inability to handle teammate-induced uncertainty. We propose a new perspective: treat teammates as structured, learnable components within the agent's world model. We introduce an architecture that factorizes the latent state of a Dreamer-style recurrent state-space model (RSSM) into environment and teammate components, and learns an auxiliary Theory-of-Mind (ToM) head to infer latent embeddings of partner behavior such as character, intent, and predicted actions from partial trajectories. These teammate latents condition the actor and critic, enabling the agent to imagine and adapt to diverse collaborators. We outline how this approach can support zero-shot and few-shot coordination in partially observable settings and propose a set of benchmarks and evaluation protocols to assess its impact. This work positions world models as not only predictors of environmental dynamics, but as simulators of social behavior, opening new directions for generalizable, human-compatible AI.

processed.summary:
- Proposes a novel **Dreamer-style architecture** that factorizes the latent state of a **Recurrent State-Space Model (RSSM)** into distinct environment and teammate components.
- Employs an auxiliary **Theory-of-Mind (ToM) head** to infer teammate intentions, characters, and actions from partial observation trajectories.
- Conditions the agent's **actor and critic** on these latent teammate embeddings to improve decision-making in collaborative, partially observable settings.
- Transforms the **world model** from a simple environment predictor into a sophisticated simulator of social behavior and social dynamics.
key_results: Factorized RSSM architecture successfully separates physical dynamics from social latent variables.
Real-time inference of teammate character and intent using a dedicated Theory-of-Mind module.
Enhanced agent adaptability through latent-conditioned actor-critic networks.
Established theoretical framework for zero-shot and few-shot coordination in MARL.
Introduction of a new evaluation protocol for assessing social intelligence within generative world models.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers propose factorizing world model latents to simulate teammate behavior, enabling better zero-shot coordination in multi-agent systems.

title: dashi: A Python library for Dataset Shift Characterization to Support Trustworthy AI Development and Deployment
summary: The Artificial Intelligence (AI) life cycle requires a thorough understanding of the underlying data dynamics for robust, safe and cost-effective AI development and use. Dataset shifts are defined as changes between train and test data distributions. Whether occurring over time (temporal) or across different sites (multi-source), they can severely degrade model performance and compromise data quality. This is particularly important in health AI, where the safety and fundamental rights of patients can be severely affected by uncontrolled shifts both at training and operational stages. While the theoretical foundations of covariate, prior, and concept shifts are well established, there is a lack of accessible and comprehensive software tools to perform their analysis. We introduce dashi, an open-source Python library designed for the exploration, quantification, and characterization of dataset shifts. dashi provides a dual approach: an unsupervised approach that leverages information geometry and non-parametric statistical manifolds to data variability characterization and analysis (e.g., Information Geometric Temporal plots and Multi-Source Variability metrics like Global Probabilistic Deviation and Source Probabilistic Outlyingness), and a supervised approach that quantifies and characterizes model performance degradation. Both unsupervised and supervised approaches work across user-defined temporal and domain/source batches. We demonstrate the utility of dashi on three simulated and real-world health AI case studies on gestational diabetes mellitus, COVID-19 and emergency medical dispatch. By providing interactive visual analytics and variability metrics, dashi supports trustworthiness of AI life cycle stages enabling robust and safe machine learning pipelines through the assessment of data coherence and AI performance.

processed.summary:
- **dashi** is a specialized Python library designed to quantify and characterize **dataset shifts**, including covariate, prior, and concept shifts, throughout the AI lifecycle.
- The architecture leverages an **unsupervised approach** based on information geometry and non-parametric statistical manifolds to analyze data variability across temporal and multi-source batches.
- It incorporates a **supervised analysis module** that maps distribution changes to actual model performance degradation, facilitating robust **AI Evaluation**.
- The framework emphasizes **Trustworthy AI** by providing interactive visual analytics and metrics like Global Probabilistic Deviation to monitor data coherence in high-stakes environments.
key_results: Development of an open-source tool for dual-pathway (supervised and unsupervised) dataset shift characterization.
Introduction of Information Geometric Temporal plots for visualizing complex data distribution changes over time.
Implementation of specific metrics such as Global Probabilistic Deviation and Source Probabilistic Outlyingness.
Demonstration of effective shift detection in three clinical case studies: GDM, COVID-19, and medical dispatch.
Establishment of a standardized workflow for assessing data variability to support safe and cost-effective AI deployment.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce dashi, an open-source Python library for quantifying dataset shifts to ensure robust and trustworthy AI deployment.

title: Diagnosing Failure Modes of Shared-State Collaboration in Resource-Constrained Visual Agents
summary: Modular visual reasoning systems increasingly rely on shared working memory for multi-step collaboration, yet the failure dynamics of intermediate state evolution in low-capacity regimes remain underexplored. We study failure modes of collaborative reasoning with weak learners (4B--8B models) through the lens of noise accumulation. We introduce CoSee, an auditing framework that formalizes the read-write-verify loop to trace information flow in document visual question answering. Across multi-page, chart, and web-based benchmarks, we find a counter-intuitive degradation: naive shared workspaces often amplify hallucinations rather than resolve them. We identify two dominant failure modes: Noise Reinforcement, where ungrounded notes are reused as evidence, and Policy Collapse, where added context shifts the model toward under-specified, short-form answers. Using cost-accuracy Pareto frontiers, we show that increased compute can correlate negatively with performance without explicit verification. Our findings suggest that for resource-constrained agents, the bottleneck lies not in reasoning depth but in communication fidelity, providing trace-level diagnostics and a mechanistic baseline for reliable modular design.

processed.summary:
* Investigates the mechanics of **shared-state collaboration** in modular visual reasoning systems using resource-constrained models (4B-8B parameters).
* Introduces **CoSee**, an auditing framework that formalizes a **read-write-verify loop** to trace information flow and identify failures in document VQA.
* Discovers that intermediate state evolution in small models frequently leads to **Noise Reinforcement** and **Policy Collapse** rather than improved accuracy.
* Demonstrates that without **explicit verification**, increased compute and reasoning steps can negatively impact the reliability of modular agent designs.
key_results: Naive shared workspaces often amplify hallucinations instead of resolving them in low-capacity models.
Noise Reinforcement occurs when ungrounded intermediate notes are recycled as valid evidence by subsequent steps.
Policy Collapse shifts model behavior toward under-specified, short-form answers as context length increases.
Cost-accuracy Pareto frontiers show that higher compute can correlate negatively with performance in unverified systems.
Communication fidelity, rather than reasoning depth, is identified as the primary bottleneck for 4B-8B visual agents.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers demonstrate that shared-state memory in small visual agents causes performance degradation through noise reinforcement and policy collapse.

title: A Visually Impaired Assistance Benchmark for VLM-as-a-Judge Evaluation
summary: AI-based Visually Impaired Assistance (VIA) remains challenging, largely due to the high cost of human evaluation. The VLM-as-a-Judge paradigm may offer a promising alternative, although it has mostly been studied in general domains. We therefore ask whether such judges can be trusted for VIA tasks. To investigate this question, we introduce VIABLE (Visually Impaired Assistance Benchmark for VLM-as-a-Judge Evaluation), the first benchmark for VLM-as-a-Judge evaluation in VIA. VIABLE contains over 300K judgment samples across three scenarios and introduces an Effectiveness--Impartiality--Stability framework with a 12-mode failure taxonomy. Based on VIABLE, our systematic study of seven judges across different model scales shows that existing models are largely unreliable across all evaluation axes. The strongest judge, GPT-5.4, achieves only 52.6% single-failure diagnostic accuracy, yet exhibits the highest self-preference rate at 94.2%; while open-source judges are strongly biased and adversarially fragile. To address these issues, we propose VIA-Judge-Agent, a model-agnostic inference-time harness that augments judges with visual evidence extraction and a taxonomy-guided workflow. It enables positive improvements in diagnostic accuracy and downstream VIA responses more preferred by BLV users. Data and code are available at: https://github.com/YiyiyiZhao/VIABLE

processed.summary:
- Introduces **VIABLE**, the first large-scale benchmark (300K+ samples) for evaluating **VLM-as-a-Judge** performance within the specialized domain of Visually Impaired Assistance (VIA).
- Establishes an **Effectiveness-Impartiality-Stability** framework coupled with a **12-mode failure taxonomy** to systematically identify why VLMs fail as objective evaluators.
- Proposes **VIA-Judge-Agent**, an inference-time harness that improves judgment reliability through **visual evidence extraction** and a taxonomy-guided reasoning workflow.
- Demonstrates that even frontier models exhibit extreme **self-preference bias** (94.2%) and poor diagnostic accuracy when assessing accessibility-focused responses.
key_results: Creation of VIABLE, a benchmark containing over 300,000 judgment samples for VIA tasks.
State-of-the-art models achieved a low 52.6% single-failure diagnostic accuracy.
Discovery of a 94.2% self-preference rate in the strongest tested proprietary model.
Open-source judges were found to be significantly biased and vulnerable to adversarial manipulation.
VIA-Judge-Agent successfully increased alignment between AI evaluations and actual Blind and Low-Vision (BLV) user preferences.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: Zhao et al. introduce VIABLE to expose and mitigate systematic biases in VLMs acting as judges for accessibility tasks.

title: FBHM: Functional Benchmarking and Steering of VLMs for Hateful Meme Detection
summary: Hateful meme detection remains a formidable challenge for vision-language models, as existing benchmarks are structurally observational - confounding rhetorical hate mechanisms with target community features and preventing causal evaluation of model vulnerabilities. To address this, we introduce FBHM, a systematically curated benchmark of Functionality Based Hateful Memes constructed along two orthogonal axes: 25 distinct rhetorical functionalities and 10 target communities (5,000 memes total). Benchmarking state-of-the-art VLMs reveals a severe generalization gap: models highly accurate on standard datasets catastrophically drop to near-random performance on FBHM, proving they exploit dataset-specific heuristics rather than robust multimodal reasoning. To efficiently close this gap, we propose LSV (learnable steering vectors), an ultra-low data regime strategy that applies a causal intervention objective on as few as 500 steering samples (50 unique base memes), boosting FBHM performance by ~30 Macro-F1 points while outperforming in-context learning and PEFT without degrading source-domain performance.

processed.summary:
- Introduces **FBHM**, a new benchmark of 5,000 memes designed to isolate **multimodal reasoning** by separating rhetorical mechanisms from target community features.
- Demonstrates that current **Vision-Language Models (VLMs)** suffer from a severe generalization gap, relying on dataset-specific heuristics rather than understanding causal rhetorical intent.
- Proposes **Learnable Steering Vectors (LSV)**, an ultra-low data intervention strategy that uses just 500 samples to guide model behavior and improve safety detection accuracy.
- Evaluates the strategy against **Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning (PEFT)** and In-Context Learning, showing superior performance in low-data regimes without catastrophic forgetting.
key_results: SOTA VLMs show near-random performance on the FBHM benchmark despite high accuracy on standard datasets.
FBHM covers 25 distinct rhetorical functionalities and 10 target communities to prevent shortcut learning.
The LSV strategy boosts Macro-F1 scores by approximately 30 points on the FBHM benchmark.
Successful steering is achieved using as few as 50 unique base memes (500 steering samples).
LSV outperforms standard ICL and PEFT methods while maintaining source-domain performance integrity.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce FBHM to benchmark and steer VLMs, closing the generalization gap in multimodal hate speech detection.

title: When Entropy Is Not Enough: Multi-Modal Classification of Encrypted and Compressed Data Fragments
summary: Reliable identification of encrypted data fragments is essential in cybersecurity, with applications to ransomware detection, digital forensics, and large-scale data analysis. Distinguishing encrypted from compressed fragments is particularly challenging, as short fragments lack structural data and exhibit low statistical redundancy. Traditional statistical methods based on byte-level distributions show limited effectiveness on this task. Recent machine learning approaches improve performance by learning subtle patterns from raw bytes, but predominantly rely on single-modal representations, implicitly assuming that a single view of the data is sufficient for accurate classification. This paper shows that this assumption becomes a fundamental limitation in low-information settings, when only small fragments of data are available (512--2048 Bytes). We propose Triumvir, a multi-modal, uncertainty-aware ensemble architecture that integrates statistical, sequential, and spatial representations of raw byte fragments. Extensive experimental analysis demonstrates that Triumvir consistently outperforms state-of-the-art methods with gains of up to +4.5pp in binary and +6.4pp in multiclass classification. Ablation studies confirm that combining modalities is critical, yielding improvements of up to +5pp over partial configurations.

processed.summary:
* Proposes **Triumvir**, a multi-modal, uncertainty-aware ensemble architecture designed to classify small encrypted and compressed data fragments.
* Integrates three distinct data representations: **statistical** (byte-level distributions), **sequential** (byte sequences), and **spatial** (image-like mappings) to overcome entropy-based limitations.
* Addresses the "low-information" constraint of 512-2048 byte fragments, which traditionally lack structural features required for standard detection.
* Enhances **cybersecurity forensics** and ransomware detection by improving classification accuracy over single-modal raw byte learning models.
key_results: Triumvir achieves up to +4.5 percentage points improvement in binary classification tasks.
Multiclass classification performance increased by up to +6.4 percentage points compared to SOTA.
Multimodal integration yielded up to +5 percentage points gain over single-representation configurations.
Successfully classifies small data fragments (512-2048 Bytes) where traditional entropy measures fail.
Demonstrates that uncertainty-aware ensemble methods are critical for low-information data settings.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: ArXiv researchers introduce Triumvir, a multi-modal ensemble that accurately classifies encrypted versus compressed data using statistical and spatial representations.

title: DecMem: Towards Minute-Long Consistent World Generation with Decoupled Memory
summary: Recent advances in video generative models have promoted rapid progress in controllable world models. However, maintaining fine-grained spatio-temporal consistency under long-horizon reasoning remains a key challenge. In this work, we move beyond explicit 3D memory and coarse frame-level implicit modeling, and propose a fine-grained, learnable, and scalable memory for consistent world generation. We first identify two fundamental limitations of naïve learnable memory architectures in long-horizon extrapolation, namely computational inefficiency and attention dispersion. Through a systematic analysis of attention dispersion, we propose DecMem, a decoupled memory architecture that employs Sparse Global Memory for efficient fine-grained access to global history and Anchored Local Memory for stable and high-quality extrapolation. Extensive experiments demonstrate that DecMem significantly outperforms current state-of-the-art methods. By ensuring precise and efficient long-term memory and achieving superior extrapolation capabilities, DecMem enables minute-level controllable long video generation with high fidelity and consistency.

processed.summary:
- Proposes **DecMem**, a decoupled memory architecture designed to solve computational inefficiency and **attention dispersion** in long-horizon world modeling.
- Introduces **Sparse Global Memory** to provide fine-grained, efficient access to global history without the overhead of dense frame-level modeling.
- Employs **Anchored Local Memory** to ensure stable extrapolation, maintaining spatio-temporal consistency across extended generation sequences.
- Enables **minute-long controllable video generation** with high fidelity, significantly outperforming existing explicit 3D and implicit memory methods.
key_results: Achieves consistent video generation at a minute-long scale.
Mitigates attention dispersion issues common in long-horizon extrapolation.
Outperforms current state-of-the-art methods in spatio-temporal consistency.
Provides a scalable alternative to coarse frame-level and explicit 3D memory architectures.
Demonstrates superior fidelity in controllable world model environments.
processed.one_sentence_takeaway: Tencent AI Lab introduces DecMem to enable minute-long consistent video generation using decoupled sparse global and anchored local memory.

title: Shi-type estimates and finite-time singularities of reasonable flows of Spin(7)-structures
summary: This paper establishes foundational analytic and geometric results for a broad class of reasonable flows of Spin($7$)-structures. We first prove Shi-type derivative estimates, showing that a uniform bound on the quantity \[ Λ(x,t)=\left(|\mathrm{Riem}(x,t)|_{g(t)}^2+|T(x,t)|_{g(t)}^4+|\nabla T(x,t)|_{g(t)}^2\right)^{1/2} \] implies bounds on all covariant derivatives of the Riemann curvature tensor $\mathrm{Riem}$ and the torsion tensor $T$. We show further that $Λ(x,t)$ must blow up at any finite-time singularity, and we establish a lower bound on the blow-up rate. We also prove a compactness theorem for solutions of such flows and apply these results to the analysis of finite-time singularities. These results provide a general analytic framework for studying flows of Spin($7$)-structures; once a proposed flow is shown to satisfy the reasonable condition, our estimates, compactness theorems, and singularity analysis apply.
